report:
## Your Health Assessment

Hi Tommy, thanks for taking the time to share this with us. Your answers reveal some meaningful connections between your cognitive concerns and digestive health that are worth exploring.

### Your Brain Fog Has a Timing Pattern Worth Noting

You mentioned that your cognitive clarity is worst after meals, and that your primary concerns are short-term memory and word-finding difficulties. This is a telling detail. When cognitive function dips specifically after eating, it often points to a [gut-brain interaction where the digestive process itself is generating signals that impair cognition](https://gut.bmj.com/content/73/7/1199). Bacterial fermentation of food can produce compounds like endotoxin and D-lactic acid that reach the brain and directly interfere with mental clarity. The fact that you also report digestive issues makes this connection even more likely: your gut and your brain fog may share the same root cause.

### Cold Hands and a Sluggish Engine

Frequently cold extremities are one of the most reliable indicators of low thyroid function, and the thyroid connection to your cognitive symptoms is hard to ignore. Research consistently shows that hypothyroidism is associated with [specific deficits in verbal memory retrieval](https://psychiatryonline.org/doi/10.1176/jnp.2007.19.2.132), which aligns closely with the word-finding difficulties you describe. The thyroid is the body's master metabolic regulator, and the brain is by far the most energy-hungry organ. When thyroid output is low, cognitive function and digestive motility both suffer, which could be linking your two primary goals more tightly than you might expect.

### Diet and Sleep May Be Working Against You

A few things stand out in your daily routine. Your smoothie breakfast likely contains a high amount of fiber and possibly seed oils, both of which can feed bacterial overgrowth and increase gut irritation. The wine at night, while relaxing, can disrupt sleep architecture and burden the liver, which plays a central role in both thyroid hormone activation and clearing gut-derived toxins. And your note that you simply don't sleep enough is significant on its own: insufficient sleep is a potent driver of stress hormones, which suppress thyroid function, worsen gut immunity, and impair the very cognitive processes you're trying to improve. These factors may be quietly sustaining the patterns above.

---

Your two goals, better gut health and sharper mental clarity, are likely more connected than they appear on the surface. A quiz like this can only surface patterns from limited data, but what we're seeing here suggests some clear threads worth exploring further.
